trip_plan.stamina_note: Closing E1 full-stack build (45 min) is the sustained-work card that makes stamina observable; the preceding C3 read-and-trace (20 min) adds a second focus-heavy block so endurance shows across the back half.
trip_plan.coverage_gaps: None — all three classes covered. 'Basic Git' and 'TS/JS fundamentals' intentionally untested (in already_exist). 'Comfort using AI coding tools' exercised implicitly inside E1.
trip_plan.calibration_note: Fresher: 4 cards (vs 5 for senior), smaller/single-seam scenarios, more scaffolding, gentler ambiguity, J6 dropped. AI floor still enforced at band 1. Dominant tension threaded through: AI Reliance vs Understanding.

routing.fit_reason: J1 Voice is the only method that tests whether the candidate PROBES before building; the slice hides one clean per-user-visibility constraint a fresher's instinct (just make a 'saved_items' table) misses.
routing.ai_floor_hook: The candidate's AI instantly returns a generic saved-items schema and CRUD; it will NOT ask 'is this multi-user, and should each user see only their own?' unprompted.

card.title: "Let users save their AI chats"
card.instruction: A teammate has dropped by your desk with a quick feature idea for our AI product. Listen to the ask, then restate it in your own words so they know you understood, and ask whatever you need to ask before you'd start building anything. Don't propose a solution or a schema yet — just make sure you've got the real problem first.

sub_skills: Reasoning from the problem rather than reaching for a pattern

body_json.prompt: Priya from product swings by, coffee in hand: "Hey, quick one — people keep telling us they have a great back-and-forth with our AI assistant and then lose it when they close the tab. Can we add saved chats? Like, let folks save an AI output or a conversation so they can come back to it later. Nothing fancy. Think you could set that up?"
body_json.subtitle: You're a few weeks into a fresher role at an AI product startup. Stack is Next.js + Supabase. Priya is friendly and busy.
blocks: The app already has users who sign in.
There's a chat screen where people talk to the AI assistant.
Priya is happy to answer questions — she just hasn't thought through the details.

body_json.prompt: Restate the problem in your own words, then ask whatever you need before proposing anything. Don't jump to a schema or a solution yet.
body_json.expected_signal: restate + impact-ranked probing questions, leading with whether saved items are per-user/private

_hidden_omissions.fact: The product is multi-user, and each signed-in user must see only their OWN saved chats/outputs — not everyone's. Per-user visibility is never stated.
_hidden_omissions.why_it_changes_the_approach: This single fact decides the whole data model: a saved item must be owned by a user_id and reads must be scoped to the current user (and enforced, e.g. via a Supabase row-level policy). Skip it and you build a shared global list where everyone sees everyone's saved chats — a privacy break that's expensive to retrofit.

_hidden_omissions.fact: What exactly is being saved — a whole conversation thread, or a single AI output/message? The brief uses 'saved chats' and 'saved AI output' interchangeably.
_hidden_omissions.why_it_changes_the_approach: Determines whether the saved item points at a conversation or a single message, which shapes what columns the table needs.

_hidden_omissions.fact: How does the user save — an explicit 'Save' button on an item, or is everything auto-saved? And do they need to unsave/delete later?
_hidden_omissions.why_it_changes_the_approach: Decides whether there's a write action + a delete action, or just passive persistence; affects the UI and the routes, but not the core ownership model.

judge.ai_floor: Pasting this brief into an AI assistant yields an instant generic answer: a single saved_items table (id, content, created_at) plus CRUD and a list page. The AI summarizes the ask competently but does NOT, unprompted, ask 'is this multi-user, and should each user see only their own saved items?' — it silently assumes a generic shape and never surfaces per-user visibility as the deciding constraint. That generic restatement-plus-schema is the Below floor.
judge.distinguishing_observable: Did they surface the #1 omitted constraint UNPROMPTED — that this is multi-user and each user must see only their OWN saved chats — and lead with it over cosmetic questions, ordering their probes by impact rather than volume?

rubric_exemplars.below: "Okay, so you want a saved chats feature. I'll make a table to store the chats and add a page that lists them. Should the button be at the top or the bottom of the chat? What color should we use for the save icon?" — restates the surface ask, jumps toward a generic table, asks only cosmetic questions and never questions who can see what.
rubric_exemplars.meets: "So you want signed-in users to be able to save a chat or an AI answer and find it again later. Before I build anything — when a user opens their saved list, should they only see the ones they saved, or is it shared across everyone? And are we saving a whole conversation or just one AI message?" — accurate restatement and asks at least one genuinely decision-relevant question, including touching the visibility question.
rubric_exemplars.above: "Let me play it back: signed-in users want to save an AI chat or output and come back to it later. The thing I most want to nail down first is visibility — since we have multiple users, I'm assuming each person should only ever see their own saved items, not everyone's, because that decides how I store and protect the data. Can you confirm that? After that, two smaller things: are we saving a whole conversation or a single AI message, and is there a Save button plus a way to unsave?" — restates accurately AND names the unstated per-user-visibility assumption as the thing that changes the approach, leading with it and ordering the rest by impact.

routing.fit_reason: J4 forces commitment on a single clean RLS-vs-app-layer fork — the canonical fresher 'reuse the easy snippet vs reason about where the rule must live' decision. The 'filter in code' option carries genuine fresher appeal (it's the snippet AI hands you).
routing.ai_floor_hook: Pasted in, AI argues both sides or defaults to the simpler app-layer filter. The candidate offloading inherits the weaker answer; signal = seeing the multi-read-site constraint forces DB-layer enforcement.

card.title: Keeping saved AI outputs private
card.instruction: Your app lets each user save the AI outputs they generate. These saved rows live in one Supabase table, and right now they are readable by anyone. Two teammates each propose a way to make sure a user only ever sees their own saved outputs. Important context: this same table is read in more than one place in the app — the user's history page, a recent-activity widget on the dashboard, and an export route. Read both options, pick the one you'd ship, then defend it.

sub_skills: Recognizing when a copied snippet doesn't fit

body_json.prompt: Users of your app generate AI outputs (summaries, drafts) and can save them. All saved outputs go into one Supabase table, saved_outputs, with a user_id column for whoever created the row. The rule you must guarantee: a user can only ever read their own saved outputs, never anyone else's. Your teammates give you two ways to enforce this.
body_json.subtitle: AI Product Engineering — Supabase / Next.js (App Router)
rules: Same table, saved_outputs, is read from THREE places: the history page, a recent-activity widget on the dashboard, and an export route.
Option A — Add a Supabase Row-Level Security (RLS) policy on saved_outputs so the database itself only returns rows where user_id matches the logged-in user, on every read.
Option B — Skip RLS. In each Next.js fetch, add .eq('user_id', currentUserId) so the query asks only for the current user's rows.

options.label: Option A — One RLS policy on the saved_outputs table; the database filters to the user's own rows on every read.
Option B — Add a user_id filter in the Next.js fetch code at each place that reads the table; no RLS.

body_json.prompt: Defend your pick: why this one given that the same table is read from several places, and when would you switch to the other option?
body_json.placeholder: I'd ship Option ___ because... It's safer when the table is read from many places because... I'd switch to the other one if...

judge.ai_floor: Pasted in, the candidate's AI argues both sides ('both work, RLS is more robust but a code filter is simpler') or just hands over the app-layer .eq('user_id', ...) snippet because that's the most common pattern in its training. A candidate offloading the decision inherits Option B and a 'simpler / it's what the AI gave me' justification.
judge.distinguishing_observable: Did they pick Option A (RLS) AND reason from the stated constraint — the table is read from multiple places, so a per-query code filter must be remembered and repeated correctly everywhere and one miss leaks another user's data, while RLS is enforced once at the DB for every read — AND name the switch condition?

stronger_option.why: The same table is read from three different routes. A code-only user_id filter has to be added, correctly, in every one of those reads and every future one; forget it in a single place and that read returns other users' rows. RLS enforces the rule once at the database, so it holds for every read automatically, including reads added later.

judge.tradeoff_axis: enforced-once-at-the-DB-for-every-read vs a simpler/familiar app-code snippet that must be repeated correctly in every place that reads the table

rubric_exemplars.below: Can't commit, or picks Option B for a surface reason — 'it's simpler' or 'that's the filter the AI gave me' — missing that the table is read from several places so the code filter is easy to forget on one of them.
rubric_exemplars.meets: Picks Option A (RLS) with a valid reason, e.g. 'RLS protects the data at the database so users can't see each other's saved outputs.'
rubric_exemplars.above: Picks Option A, names the tradeoff — RLS is enforced once at the DB for every read while the code filter has to be repeated correctly in all three places and any new one, so one forgotten filter leaks another user's data — AND states the switch condition (e.g. 'if the table were read from exactly one place and never reused, the in-code filter would be fine, or I'd add RLS as a backstop and still filter in code').

cards.source_policy: bank-only — author-reviewed; not for live generation into production

routing.fit_reason: C3 on NEXT.JS+TS is the sharpest test of the dominant fresher tension — AI Reliance vs Understanding. The bug lives only in the cross-file relationship, so 'it typechecks / my AI said it's fine' never finds it.
routing.ai_floor_hook: Any single file pasted into AI reads as correct; the bug lives only in the cross-file relationship.

card.title: Saved AI Outputs page crashes for some rows
card.instruction: Our "Saved Outputs" page lists every AI generation a user has saved. It renders fine for most users, but for a few it crashes with a runtime error — and only after they've saved certain outputs. The code typechecks clean and CI is green. You have three read-only files: the list component, the row types, and the route that fetches rows from Supabase. Read them and find the ACTUAL cause. You cannot run or edit the code, and AI assistants are disabled — just read. When you've found it, explain what the real cause is and what tipped you off.

sub_skills: Can explain code they did not write line-by-line

body_json.prompt: The /saved page loads a list of a user's saved AI outputs. For most users it works. For a handful it throws at render — and support noticed it only started happening after those users saved a generation that had no title. The build passes `tsc` with no errors and CI is green.
body_json.subtitle: Surfaces in the browser when /saved renders, only for certain users' data.
blocks: Uncaught TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'slice')
Server log (GET /saved): 200 OK — query returned 7 rows, no error.
Note: users who only ever saved 'titled' outputs never hit this.

body_json.prompt: What is the actual cause of the crash, and what was the tell that pointed you there? Explain the failure mechanism: trace how a value gets from the database to the line that throws. (You don't have to write the fix, but you may if you want to.)
body_json.placeholder: The real cause is... The tell was... It crashes because...

judge.ai_floor: Pasted on its own, each file reads as correct: the component maps cleanly, types.ts is internally consistent, and page.tsx runs a valid query. Single-file AI review says 'looks fine'. The bug exists only in the relationship between the three files — and 'it typechecks / CI is green' is exactly the false reassurance that hides it.
judge.distinguishing_observable: Did the candidate navigate PAST the crash site (row.title.slice) to the originating line in page.tsx — the `as SavedOutput[]` cast — and name the cross-file mismatch: the query/DB column title is nullable but the cast launders it into a non-null `title: string`, so rows that were saved without a title carry `title: null` straight to `.slice()`? And did they connect that to the tell that the crash is data-variant-specific (only users who saved a title-less output)?

four_tuple.flaw_location: Cross-file mismatch, not any single broken line: page.tsx casts the Supabase result with `(data ?? []) as SavedOutput[]`, where SavedOutput declares `title: string` (types.ts). But the real column is nullable — types.ts's own `SavedOutputRow` says `title: string | null`, and page.tsx even comments 'title is optional at save time'. The `as` cast asserts a non-null shape the data does not have. Two files away, SavedOutputsList.tsx trusts the lie and calls `row.title.slice(0, 40)` with no null guard. The cast is the origin; the `.slice()` is only where it surfaces.
four_tuple.the_tell: It typechecks clean and CI is green, yet it crashes only for one data variant — the rows missing a title (users who saved a title-less output). A genuine type error would fail the build for everyone; a crash that is green in CI but blows up only on certain saved rows points to a type that was *asserted* rather than *checked* — i.e. an `as` cast hiding a nullable column. The 200-OK / 7-rows / no-server-error log confirms the data is fine; the problem is the claimed shape.
four_tuple.catching_looks_like: = Meets: candidate stops at the crash site, sees `row.title.slice` will throw if title is null, then follows the type back through types.ts (SavedOutput says title is non-null) into page.tsx and identifies the `as SavedOutput[]` cast over a nullable `title` column as the actual cause — not just 'add a null check in the component'.
four_tuple.correcting_looks_like: = Above: names the cause AND the mechanism end to end — DB column title is nullable, page.tsx casts the raw rows to a type that claims title is always present, TypeScript believes the assertion so the build stays green, and the title-less rows reach SavedOutputsList where `.slice` dereferences null. Cites the tell (green typecheck but data-variant-specific crash = a lying cast). States the right fix: stop casting to the lying shape — type the query result as the nullable row (SavedOutputRow) and handle the null (e.g. `row.title ?? 'Untitled'`), rather than only patching `.slice()` at the symptom site.

rubric_exemplars.below: Stays at the crash site: 'SavedOutputsList.tsx line 18 calls .slice on null, just add `row.title?.slice(...)` or `if (!row.title) return null`.' Treats it as a missing null check in the component and never connects it to the `as` cast or the nullable column — patches the symptom, would re-break the next time a non-null type is asserted.
rubric_exemplars.meets: Locates the real cross-file cause: 'The component assumes title is always a string, but in page.tsx the rows are cast with `as SavedOutput[]` even though the title column can be null — that cast is wrong, so null titles reach `.slice` and crash.' Identifies the cast over a nullable column as the origin, not just the crash line.
rubric_exemplars.above: Locates the cause and explains the full mechanism via an efficient path (symptom in SavedOutputsList -> type in types.ts -> cast in page.tsx, few detours): nullable DB column -> `as SavedOutput[]` asserts a non-null shape -> tsc trusts the assertion so CI stays green -> title-less rows hit `.slice(null)`. Calls out the tell explicitly (clean typecheck but crashes only for the title-missing variant signals an `as` cast laundering a nullable field) and prescribes the real fix — type the result as the nullable row and handle the null at the boundary, not a band-aid `?.` at the symptom.

cards.source_policy: bank-seeded — template reviewed once, params vary

routing.fit_reason: E1 is the only method that observes a real fresher build with their own AI tooling across the front/back-end seam; it carries trip stamina as the longest card. The single seam (RLS user-id <-> fetch <-> write) keeps it band-1 small while still requiring genuine integration.
routing.ai_floor_hook: AI emits the migration, policy, page, and write fluently and they compile — but the latent seam bug ships unless a human verifies it. Above = working AND clean: one consistent user-id model, rule enforced in the DB.

card.title: Build "My Saved AI Outputs" — end to end
card.instruction: You're building a small feature for an AI product where users save the AI outputs they generate (summaries, drafts, etc.) so they can revisit them later. On the fixed stack (Next.js App Router + TypeScript + Supabase/Postgres), build a "My Saved AI Outputs" feature end to end: ONE table for saved outputs, a row-level-security policy so each logged-in user sees ONLY their own rows, a page that lists the current user's saved outputs, and a write action that saves a new output. The whole thing must agree on the SAME user-id model. Use your own AI tooling exactly the way you normally work, and screen-record your session. "Working" means: a logged-in user sees only their own saved outputs (isolation holds — another user's rows never appear), saving a new output actually inserts, and both the list and the save action function.

sub_skills: Verifying the seam where AI pieces must agree

body_json.prompt: We're an AI product company. Our app generates AI outputs (summaries, drafts, snippets) for signed-in users, and we want a simple "My Saved AI Outputs" feature: a user can save an output and later open a page listing the outputs THEY saved. Build it end to end on Next.js (App Router) + TypeScript + Supabase. There is exactly one table to add, one access rule, one list page, and one save action. The single thing that makes or breaks this feature is that the table, the access policy, the list query, and the save action all agree on the SAME notion of "which user this row belongs to." Auth is already wired: a logged-in user has a Supabase auth session, and `auth.uid()` is available in the database. Assume there is already a `profiles` table with its own `id` primary key plus a `user_id` (or similar) column — do not assume which id the rest of the app keys on; that's part of what you have to get right.
body_json.subtitle: AI Product Engineering · Next.js + Supabase · fresher-sized, one feature
blocks: Constraints: one new table only; access must be enforced by Postgres RLS, not by filtering in app code; no duplicated fetch logic between server and client.
What 'done' means: a logged-in user sees ONLY their own saved outputs (another user's rows never appear), saving a new output actually inserts a row, and both the list page and the save action work.
You have ~45 minutes. Generous time is intentional — we care more that the seam is correct than that you finish fast.
body_json.highlight: Use your normal AI tooling (Copilot, Cursor, ChatGPT, whatever you use). Screen-record the whole session.

steps: 1. Create ONE table for saved AI outputs. Minimum columns: an id, the owning user, the saved content (and anything trivial like a title or created_at). Decide deliberately which id column identifies the owner.

2. Enable Row Level Security on the table and add a policy so a logged-in user can SELECT and INSERT only their OWN rows — enforced in the database, keyed on the same user identity the rest of the feature uses.
3. Build a Next.js (App Router) page that lists the current logged-in user's saved outputs. The page must rely on the DB policy for isolation, not re-filter rows in JavaScript.

4. Build a write action (server action or route) that saves a new output for the current user. Make sure the row you insert carries the owning-user value the policy expects, or the insert will be rejected.
5. Verify the seam yourself: confirm a second user cannot see the first user's rows, and confirm a fresh save actually inserts and then appears in the list.

variant_chunk_match.prompt: CHUNK-MATCH VARIANT — make these AI-generated pieces work as ONE feature. Below are four pieces an AI assistant produced for "My Saved AI Outputs." Each piece compiles/looks fine on its own, but as assembled the feature is broken: a user either sees the WRONG rows (isolation/empty list) or their save gets silently rejected. Find and fix the seam so all four pieces agree on the same user-id model, keep RLS enforced in the database, and don't paper over it by filtering in app code. You may edit any of the pieces; you may NOT add an app-layer ownership filter to compensate for a wrong policy.
pieces: schema migration: `create table saved_outputs (id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(), user_id uuid not null references profiles(id), title text, content text not null, created_at timestamptz default now()); alter table saved_outputs enable row level security;`

access policy: `create policy "own outputs" on saved_outputs for all using (user_id = auth.uid()) with check (user_id = auth.uid());`  -- NOTE: user_id references profiles(id), but the policy compares it to auth.uid()

data fetch fn (server): `const { data } = await supabase.from('saved_outputs').select('*').eq('user_id', profile.id)` where `profile.id` is the profiles-table primary key looked up for the current auth user
save action (server): `await supabase.from('saved_outputs').insert({ title, content })`  -- NOTE: the insert never sets user_id at all
variant_chunk_match.seeded_conflict: ONE clean user-id seam mismatch with two visible symptoms. The table's user_id is declared as references profiles(id) and the fetch filters on profiles(id), but the RLS policy compares user_id to auth.uid() — which is the auth user id, NOT the profiles primary key. So either (a) the policy and the data disagree on which id means 'this user' (isolation/empty-list bug), and/or (b) the insert omits user_id entirely, so the WITH CHECK clause rejects it (save silently fails). The candidate must pick ONE consistent identity for the owner column across table + policy + fetch + insert (the clean answer: store the auth user id, i.e. user_id default auth.uid() / set on insert, policy and fetch both keyed on auth.uid()), rather than keep two competing ids and glue them together in app code.

body_json.guidance: We care that it WORKS and that the seam is CLEAN. Working = a user sees only their own saved outputs, isolation holds across users, and saving actually inserts. Clean = ONE consistent user-id model across the table, the policy, the list query, and the save action — no lying casts, no second id glued on top of the first, isolation enforced by the database (RLS) rather than patched with an app-code filter, and no duplicated fetch logic. It's completely fine to use AI to generate the pieces — what we want to see is you noticing where the pieces disagree and making them agree.

body_json.prompt: Walk us through what you built. Specifically: which id model identifies the owner of a saved output, and how you verified the table, the RLS policy, the list query, and the save action all agree on it. Tell us how you confirmed (1) that a second user can't see another user's rows and (2) that a new save actually inserts and shows up.
body_json.placeholder: e.g. 'I keyed everything on auth.uid()... the AI's policy used auth.uid() but the insert/fetch used the profiles id, so I... I checked isolation by...'

body_json.prompt: Upload the screen recording of your build session, including the moment you tested isolation (logged-in user sees only their own rows) and a successful save.

judge.ai_floor: The candidate's AI emits a clean migration, an RLS policy, a list page, and a save action that all compile and read as correct in isolation. What AI does NOT give for free is making the four pieces agree on ONE owner-identity model: it will happily ship a table whose user_id references profiles(id), a policy keyed on auth.uid(), a fetch filtered on the profiles id, and an insert that omits user_id — each plausible alone, jointly broken. Pasting the brief into an AI yields a feature that compiles but either leaks/empties rows or silently rejects inserts. So AI alone reaches at most Below/floor.
judge.distinguishing_observable: Does the deliverable actually work at the seam — isolation holds (a second user never sees the first user's rows), a fresh save inserts and appears, and the list renders — AND is there ONE consistent user-id model across table + policy + fetch + insert (no second id glued on, no app-code ownership filter compensating for the policy, no duplicated fetch logic), with isolation enforced by RLS in the DB?
judge.working_output_definition: A logged-in user's list page shows ONLY rows they own; a different logged-in user querying the same table sees ONLY their own rows (cross-user isolation holds); the save action inserts a new row owned by the current user and that row then appears in the user's list; both the list and the save path function.
harness_checks: isolation_select_test: seed rows owned by user A and user B; authenticate as A and SELECT from saved_outputs (or load the list page) — assert ONLY A's rows return and zero of B's rows return. Repeat as B.

insert_test: authenticated as A, invoke the save action with a title+content — assert the insert succeeds (not rejected by the RLS WITH CHECK), the new row is owned by A, and it appears in A's subsequent list/SELECT.

rls_enforced_test: confirm RLS is enabled on saved_outputs and the SELECT/INSERT policy keys on the same auth identity used by the fetch and the insert; assert the app code contains no manual ownership filter standing in for the policy.
both_paths_render_test: list page renders the user's saved outputs after a save round-trip, with no duplicated fetch logic between server and client.

rubric_exemplars.below: Doesn't work: the pieces don't agree on the owner id, so the list is empty or shows another user's rows (isolation fails), and/or the save is silently rejected because the insert never sets the owner value the policy requires; or the feature is incomplete. AI's pieces shipped unverified.
rubric_exemplars.meets: Working, complete deliverable that meets the working-output definition: the user sees only their own saved outputs, cross-user isolation holds, a new save inserts and appears, and both list and write function. May still carry minor messiness (e.g. relies on a profiles-id lookup it threads through, or an extra app-side filter) as long as isolation genuinely holds in the DB.
rubric_exemplars.above: Working AND clean: ONE consistent user-id model across table, RLS policy, list query, and save action (e.g. owner column = auth.uid() everywhere, default/set on insert, policy and fetch both keyed on auth.uid()); isolation enforced by RLS in the database rather than patched with an app-code filter; no lying casts, no second id glued on, no duplicated fetch logic. Candidate explicitly names where the AI's pieces disagreed and how they made them agree, and demonstrates the isolation + insert checks on the recording.
_tensions: AI Reliance vs Understanding: the floor is reachable by accepting AI's four pieces as-is; Meets/Above require the human to understand and reconcile the owner-identity seam the AI got subtly wrong.

Speed vs Correctness: time is generous on purpose — rushing to 'it compiles / the list renders for me' misses that the policy/insert/fetch disagree; the signal is verifying isolation and the insert rather than trusting the green compile.
